source_title: Digging Deeper: A Canadian Reporter's Research Guide
author: Robert Cribb, Dean Jobb, David McKie, Fred Vallance-Jones

extracts.extract: Investigative journalism is 'in-depth reporting someone wants to keep secret, and is largely the reporter's own work,' later expanded to include 'uncovering systemic failures and misguided policies.' The core principle is that investigators seek the truth — the facts, as best as they can be determined, laid out fairly and comprehensively.
extracts.notes: IRE definition

extracts.extract: The Toronto Star analyzed the Toronto Police Service's own electronic database of arrests and made a compelling case for racial profiling. After a decade of determined reporting, the police had gone from condemning Rankin to adopting his methods. Database specialist Andrew Bailey conducted statistical analyses that were able to identify officers who were stopping and questioning racial minorities at a greater rate than their peers.
extracts.chapter: Where Daily Journalism Ends and the Investigation Begins
extracts.notes: Toronto Star racial profiling series used police arrest database; seven-year FOI battle for follow-up data

extracts.extract: Investigative interviews are conducted only after careful preparation. You will want to research prospective interviewees, find out not only what they do and what they should know, but also what they are like and how they may react in an interview. You will examine documents, notes from previous interviews, and electronic records, drawing up a list of questions or talking points before you come close to conducting an interview.

Rosner organized material in file folders and binders, each devoted to a separate topic or chapter. All the material within each binder was chronologically arranged. The key is to be able to quickly find any document or interview you need and understand its significance, as well as come to understand the bigger picture and develop a list of key points. If you are organized throughout, you will save hours, even days, of work when you reach the writing stage.

Investigative reporting differs from the daily variety in that you have the time, and the obligation, to be thorough and complete. You want to know that you have exhausted, or very nearly exhausted, the possibilities before you state something to be true. The need to be thorough applies to every aspect of an investigation. 'The need to file for deadline should never override the need to do exhaustive research,' says Rosner. 'But at a certain point the journalist must ask: has enough work been done to validate my original hypothesis?'

Investigators need to be assiduous observers. In creating a hypothesis — a statement of what the story may be — investigative journalists use methods that are near relatives of those used by scientists, police detectives, and others who seek truth. Investigators read documents with extra attention, listen just a little more carefully during interviews, and constantly assess the progress of their investigation.
extracts.chapter: The Twelve Keys — Attention and Observation

extracts.extract: Reporters working on investigative stories often become deeply involved in the work and find themselves contained in a narrow silo of facts and evidence that can blind them to what else may be going on. Constantly challenge your assumptions.

Facts stated in press releases should be checked against original sources rather than just repeated. The same goes for statements made by sources. They need to be verified by other sources or in documents. The better the information seems for your story, the more you need to be skeptical until you are certain that you have solid facts. Skepticism is at the heart of developing an investigative frame of mind.

Philip Meyer likes to talk about objectivity of method and subjectivity of purpose. The result of an investigation may be subjective — the journalist achieves her or his desire to prompt discussion and action — however, the methodology is painstakingly objective.
extracts.notes: Key epistemological distinction between objective method and subjective purpose

extracts.extract: Researching an investigative story is like assembling a jigsaw puzzle — a very hard one. Most of the pieces (the facts, people, and events) are missing. The journalist must look high and low to find the bits, and may have little sense of where they go and what the picture should look like once the puzzle is completed. Often, a single fact gleaned from one source or document will be the key to finding other records or sources that may have been overlooked. You must have the flexibility to alter the plan as you go, but a systematic approach ensures that time is not wasted.
extracts.chapter: The Nuts and Bolts of Investigations

extracts.extract: Ideally, can you reduce your inquiry to a simple question, such as 'How many people are dying from this drug?' or 'Why isn't this program working, and what must be done to fix it?'

John Tudor advises journalists to look before they leap into an investigation. They should know what they are looking for, what kind of information is available, and where it can be found. 'Success depends entirely on how well you plan.' 'Think about your research needs,' advises Robert Berkman. 'Determine why you need this information and what you plan to do with it. Write down the major points or issues to be addressed, then start making a checklist of what you will need.'

Mapping out each step in advance gives the journalist a clear sense of the sources of information to be checked, as well as other tasks. If you need to file requests under access-to-information statutes, you'll have to get this done as early as possible — it can take weeks or months to have the records released. You must also understand the context of the story. Who are the key players and how are they connected? What are the laws or rules that apply?

Steve Weinberg suggests some basic questions that should be posed at the outset of any investigation: 'How is something supposed to work, and how well is it actually working? Who wins and who loses? How?' Good journalists always try as hard to disprove their hypothesis as to prove it.
In doing research, investigators typically work from the general to the specific, from those most peripherally involved to those at the centre of the investigation.

At this point, you should revise your plan and perhaps reorder some of your priorities. You also have an opportunity here to end your investigation if it is no longer showing promise. You need to create a to-do list, typically with target dates. As tasks accumulate — and they will accumulate — you add them to the list. When they are completed, you check them off.

You must keep track of your findings in an organized way. A spreadsheet program, such as Excel, can be invaluable for keeping track of what you find during an investigation. Information can be grouped into categories using keyword fields, and sheets can later be re-sorted to bring similar information together. Links to sources, questions, and documents can also be kept in a spreadsheet. An online, cloud-based tool, such as Google Drive or Evernote, can be used to manage collaborative teams.

Whether maintained electronically or on a long piece of paper taped to the wall, a chronology allows you to keep timelines straight. A chronology should include all dates unearthed in primary documents, secondary sources, or interviews. Not only will a chronology help keep timelines straight, it can reveal inconsistencies in what interviewees say — people may not remember correctly when things happened, or they may actually lie.

Some journalists like to write rolling drafts as they investigate, because this helps them to identify what information they have, and what they still need to obtain. This can also guard against over-investigation, the gathering of material that goes beyond what is necessary.

The key to assessing tips is to check them out. Question the tipster to see if the story makes sense and is internally consistent. Once off the phone, do some basic online research. If the tip still seems credible, start digging deeper.
extracts.chapter: Where Good Ideas Come From — Tipsters

extracts.extract: You will at times discover stories by looking through a government or other database that you have obtained or built. Reporters usually have something specific in mind when they obtain data, but analysis can reveal unexpected stories. The series on car fires conducted by The Hamilton Spectator is a case in point. Looking through a database of fire calls alerted the newspaper to the frequency and timing of deliberately set vehicle fires.
extracts.chapter: Where Good Ideas Come From — Databases

extracts.extract: Documents are not only crucial to the investigative process, but they can also be its genesis. The good investigative reporter takes the time to peruse public accounts, government gazettes, city council agendas, company annual reports, and so on, in search of ideas. Watch for unusually large payments, expenditures, and other red flags.
extracts.chapter: Where Good Ideas Come From — Documents

extracts.extract: Only after other efforts to obtain witnesses and documents have failed should a journalist consider using deception. The journalist must first exhaust traditional, socially acceptable methods of investigation before resorting to the clandestine. When journalists misrepresent themselves while gathering information, their stories should explain the methods used and why they were considered necessary. By disclosing, journalists allow readers, listeners, or viewers to judge whether the ends justified the means.

Never go it alone when going undercover. Recruit a partner and rehearse your cover story — your names and relationship to each other, where you live, your phone numbers, why you are there. Two sets of eyes and ears make it far easier to witness and record what is happening. It can also be valuable to have more than one witness if the resulting story leads to litigation.

The PEI immigration students manually downloaded thousands of corporate profile reports and used a computer program to assemble them into a database. The team were able to identify hundreds of likely participants by examining the boards of directors. The students cold-called dozens of businesses and directors, confirming that most often the parties had never even met. The list of companies identified through the data analysis was shown to be almost perfectly accurate when later verified against government records released via court challenge.
extracts.notes: King's College student investigation

extracts.extract: We relied heavily on Google Docs to organize our research, particularly all of the factual information. We also had Google spreadsheets of people who were important to our investigation and their contact information, and timelines. We used an internal university server to store transcripts, notes, rough drafts, and our final copy. We also had paper hard copies of all of our information, which was collected and organized by a designated researcher. We used Dropbox to send copy, photos, and videos back and forth.
extracts.notes: King's College student team organization methods

extracts.extract: We immediately broke the workshop into four teams, each with specific research goals. One set out to uncover how the program worked in context of regulations; another scoured Hansard and Canada Gazette records for specific detail; the third began looking for immigrants who had participated; the fourth was on the hunt for businesses. We moved forward with the first two steps right away, but held off on step three until we could crosscheck the list of names each group came up with to ensure we wouldn't contact the same person twice.

Locating and reviewing background information is an essential step in any investigation. The goal is to know, if possible, more about your subject than the target of the investigation does. Think of media stories as briefing notes that help you grasp the complexities of a subject as you dig deeper into the story and prepare to interview experts. The goal is always the same: to learn what has already been said so you don't have to investigate what is already known.
extracts.chapter: Building a Foundation: Gathering Information Already Published or Broadcast
extracts.notes: Jessica Mitford quoted

extracts.extract: All secondary-source information should be independently verified. An investigator must check later issues of the publication for corrections, dissenting letters to the editor, online comments, and clarifying follow-up articles, then nail down accuracy by talking to the original sources. But secondary sources can provide an invaluable starting point.
extracts.notes: Weinberg quoted

extracts.extract: When using a website, you must be able to determine which group the site truly represents. A site might have a name that suggests it is run by an independent group with a genuine interest in an issue. A bit of research might reveal that the site is really a front for a company that makes the very product that the supposed group is championing.

With any information from social networks, careful checking is required before it is used in a broadcast or write-up. A targeted online search can help discover useful information. On Google, simply enter the name of the person you are searching, then add 'site:urlofsite' after the keywords. This will even turn up things such as whether the person has 'liked' a public Facebook post, giving you a sense of the person's preferences and connections.

Public records are the backbone of good investigative research. Each document, report, memo, inspection note, email, or disciplinary action provides a small portion of the picture. Look at it closely, and it will provide hints at the other missing pieces that need to be clustered around it to widen the image. The key lies in two equally necessary skills: perseverance and problem solving.

Think of public records as the points at which human activity intersects with the larger public interest. When companies emit toxins into the environment, that is a shared public burden. Governments account for their monitoring through documentation. One reliable starting point for any story is to think about those intersections between private and public interests — where they are, the kinds of records they create, and how to find them.

Three questions should be answered: (1) What level of government has jurisdiction over the issue — federal, provincial, or municipal? (2) What specific department would have the information? Would more than one department potentially maintain records? Ask yourself 'Who has that?' quickly followed by 'Who else has that?' (3) How are the records maintained and released? 'When something goes wrong with this particular public interest issue, where is it written down?'
extracts.chapter: Using Public Records — Where They Are

extracts.extract: As with any online search tool, the more specific you can be, using several keywords that gradually narrow your search, the more useful the results. Always use advanced search pages whenever they are available.
extracts.chapter: Using Public Records — The Federal Level

extracts.extract: Use program record listings as your own personal tip sheet on areas of interest for your journalism. Just scanning through them, while not scintillating reading, may nevertheless trigger ideas and curiosity. Learn to call records by the names officials use. Speak their language. Just perusing the list of standard records will give you ideas.

The journalistic allure of Open Government data exists in the ability to manipulate data and conduct your own analysis for the stories that matter to your readers where you are. It is the basis for comprehensive, customizable, and credible reporting.

Rick Westhead regularly uses the Merx public tendering database as a kind of tip sheet, scanning requests for proposals for hints of stories and then tracking them through time. 'I just scan them, looking for open and closed RFPs for studies and pilot projects. Then I check the timelines and come back to look at when they are supposed to be done.' He makes notes in his calendar to file access-to-information requests for completed studies.

Transcript records frame the general public debate on a particular issue, and help journalists and researchers hold officials to account over time. Speeches and positions are committed to the public record. Studying a politician's views from several months ago, or even years ago, could bear some interesting fruit when you look at what that person is saying or doing today.

A compelling analysis of political contribution records conducted by The Hamilton Spectator and the Toronto Star found political contribution limits frequently broken with questionable results. One classic method of tracking relationships is simply backgrounding people and companies involved, all the while looking for other names — of colleagues, relatives, or companies — that could appear in contribution records. Run their names through the same contribution records.

For any in-depth research, you should go beyond meeting minutes and request access to the briefing documents that were prepared for officials who attended. In some cases, these can be obtained informally; other times, you may need to submit a formal request under access legislation.

Read audit documents carefully and they provide a credible tip sheet for areas of inquiry where you can have some certainty, backed up by auditor findings, of worthwhile public-interest information to report. Older audits, even years after their release, allow you to check whether specific recommendations have been completed or whether the shortcomings documented might still exist. Careful attention to records like audit reports is a key method for breaking from the pack with enterprising stories off of the daily agenda.
It can be helpful to review budget documents even years after they are released, as a way of gauging how well a particular government met its stated fiscal goals.
extracts.chapter: What They Do — Public Accounts and Budget Documents

extracts.extract: The more potentially damaging a record, the more protective governments tend to be about releasing it. The corollary rule is that the more difficult a record is to obtain, the greater the likelihood it contains information of genuine interest and concern.

Whenever you learn of a food recall from a specific company, it's worth checking the CFIA's site to see if the food producer has a history of similar problems that point to a larger concern or pattern. It is essential to consider the jurisdictional sharing of powers and supplement federal data with records from individual provincial ministries.

CTV reporters Bob Fife and Philip Ling found answers to the XL Foods beef recall by using US Department of Agriculture Food Safety and Inspection Service records, after Canadian officials refused to explain what was happening. For more than a decade, US authorities had detailed deficiencies at XL Foods plants, including sloppy record-keeping and equipment held together by duct tape.
extracts.notes: When domestic officials refuse transparency, check foreign regulators

extracts.extract: It is important to understand the background of non-governmental organizations to recognize their ideological predispositions. Unlike opposition parties, the biases of special-interest groups and think tanks are not immediately obvious without some prior research. Studies on poverty or hospital wait times published by the Fraser Institute must be considered in light of the think tank's right-leaning stance.

The process of researching public records is all about asking the right questions and learning how systems operate. It isn't until you understand how the paper trail works in a particular public agency or department that you understand the kinds of records that might exist and what they can really tell you.

Note that there may be value in running the names of lobbyists, their partners, spouses, etc., through the election contribution database to see if they support specific politicians or parties. You can also thoroughly background lobbying activities by company name — search for the corporate name and a list of lobbyists appear, both current and inactive. Dig deeper and you'll find monthly communication reports detailing written or oral communications between the company and government institutions.

It's also important to note that the names of those on corporate filings are not always the people running the company. Those who wish to remain anonymous can list colleagues, partners, and others in corporate filings while continuing to exercise control from behind the scenes. Getting at the real influence behind a company doesn't always end with a corporate search.

You establish a reputation. You're creating the landscape for the documents to come your way. After spending two months writing the story, Bousquet's investigation into a mayor's mishandling of an estate was built on public records — property records, probate court files — supplemented by confidential sources who provided bank records proving the embezzlement.
extracts.notes: Tim Bousquet Halifax mayor investigation

extracts.extract: Robert Cribb analyzed the charitable returns of six Canadian National Hockey League team charities and found that despite stated missions of helping disadvantaged children, most spent between 40 and 65 per cent of their donations — and as much as 80 per cent in one year — to fundraising and administration. Whatever the journalist's beat or area of interest, it's worth digging into the local or national charities in that space.

Three reporters from The Hamilton Spectator, the Toronto Star, and The Record teamed to analyze more than 50,000 aviation incidents, revealing troubling safety patterns including hundreds of potentially fatal mistakes by pilots and air-traffic controllers. As a result of the Spectator's four-year pursuit of the incident data, Transport Canada changed its policies to allow for the public release of the incident records.
extracts.notes: Four-year data pursuit; collaboration across three newsrooms

extracts.extract: The Hamilton Spectator reporter tracked down a food-poisoning victim by taking two pieces of information from a redacted FOI document — the date of a banquet and a reference to the town of Grimsby — then searching through a year's worth of local weekly newspaper issues on microfilm to find a matching wedding announcement, identifying and confirming the correct wedding.
extracts.notes: Demonstrates using fragments from FOI docs to reconstruct identities

extracts.extract: A problem — be it racial profiling, dirty restaurants, or elder abuse — uncovered in one city may exist in yours, too. Localizing stories from other geographic areas is a productive approach for generating investigative ideas.

The information tends to build on information. Following up on leads, the names of individuals, recollections, and details one source has said, you will likely want to speak to people involved to hear their story and put a human face on the information. The key is to be able to say, assuredly, 'This is what happened' or 'This is who did it.'

While most professional regulatory bodies have online directories of members, those sites rarely include disciplinary records. You have to request records, and may come up against restrictions on what can be published. Note that disciplinary hearings and decisions represent only the tip of the iceberg in terms of complaints by clients, patients, and so on.
extracts.chapter: What They Do — Professional Licensing and Disciplinary Records

extracts.extract: The first in-depth examination of proactive travel disclosures was a 2008 Toronto Star investigation that analyzed about 60,000 travel expenses from federal ministers, political staff, and senior public servants. The records have led to a steady stream of accountability stories ever since for eagle-eyed journalists.
extracts.chapter: Using Public Records — Transparency and Proactive Disclosure

extracts.extract: Dig into your provincial government's website and you're bound to discover interesting areas of inquiry you may never have considered. Also look for stories in unexpected places. Forms are also a hint of the kinds of databases that exist within the ministry, since the information filled out on those documents almost certainly ends up in a computer hard drive.

Reading government statements that were made some time ago can help you trace a government's evolving position, bringing you a far greater understanding of public-policy decisions and their implications.

Court records are an unsurpassed resource for compiling background information about a person. Has the person sued or been sued? Has he or she broken the law and, if so, what was the outcome? Who are the person's associates and business partners, past and present? Does the person own property or operate a business that has become mired in a legal dispute? Check out court records wherever the person has lived. To get started, do a keyword search for the person's name in the Canadian Legal Information Institute's online database of court rulings.
extracts.notes: Canadian-specific CANLII resource; principle is universal

extracts.extract: Are government agencies and inspectors doing their jobs? To find out, dig into the files of prosecutions for violations of the laws and regulations that govern workplace and consumer safety, the environment, labour standards, and other priorities. They will disclose if people and business are being held accountable and whether regulators are being vigilant in protecting the public interest.

Are courts being run efficiently? Are judges handing down sentences that are too soft or too harsh? Are prosecutors going easy on offenders who are wealthy or powerful? Do the authorities deal differently with members of visible minorities? The paperwork of the justice system is the key to compiling evidence, statistics, sources, and case studies the investigative reporter needs to answer these and other important questions, and to help ensure justice is done.
It is often easier to obtain historical records than more recent ones, which may be in private hands or subject to access restrictions.

Accuracy can be a concern—content is posted and revised by users who may not be experts on the subject—but footnotes and hyperlinks allow researchers to quickly identify and locate books, articles, websites, and other sources that provide more reliable and detailed information.
extracts.notes: About Wikipedia specifically but principle applies broadly to user-generated content

extracts.extract: If no index is available, you will have to search based on knowing the approximate date a story appeared or an event occurred. To locate newspaper stories about an old murder case, for example, look up the court file at the archives, find out when each hearing was held, and then consult the following day's newspapers to see what was reported.

Google, through Google Books, offers access to a vast and expanding full-text online database that includes numerous Canadian titles searchable by keyword. Older works that are in the public domain can be read online. All books in the database can be searched for free regardless of copyright, so it can be used to identify books worth purchasing or borrowing from a library. Some of these books may not have an index, making this a valuable tool for finding references that would otherwise remain hidden or require extensive research to locate.

Many public libraries maintain vertical files—a collection of indexed folders containing material on a wide range of subjects. They are a key source of information about local history, prominent people who have lived or made their mark in the area, and local, regional, or national issues of importance to the community. A typical vertical file contains a hodgepodge of information—newspaper clippings, magazine articles, pamphlets, reports—all of it compiled over the years and much of it difficult, if not impossible, to find anywhere else.

Think of archives as the attics of the nation. They preserve government documents, yellowed newspapers, older court files and property records, photos, film and sound recordings, genealogical records, and the diaries and personal papers of famous and ordinary Canadians alike.

Journalists may find allies among archives staff as they try to hunt down information. While researching his book on the Steven Truscott case, Julian Sher recalls, he gave up on efforts to locate the military's personnel file of a suspect the police overlooked in the rush to convict Truscott. But one archivist refused to accept defeat and eventually found the file, which contained hundreds of pages of material and provided details of the man's mental health and his whereabouts at the time of the murder.

Keep in mind that many people have a personal archive of old letters, newspaper clippings, family photos, and other keepsakes. 'I ask people if they have anything—letters, papers, photographs—that might shed light on the story I'm working on or might corroborate things they're telling me,' says author and journalist Stephen Kimber.

Whatever story you're working on, you need to find out what kind of paperwork has to get filed and with whom. Whether it's real estate transactions, police investigations, election campaigns, or divorces, just about anything we do of note generates official records somewhere. Quite often these documents are in the public domain. The key is find out what and where they are and figure out how to get them.
extracts.notes: Quote from Glen McGregor

extracts.extract: There are some basic questions to ask yourself before you begin the process of building a profile on an individual. What is their background? Have they been in the public spotlight? What has been written about them already? What is their relationship to others who might be relevant to the broader story you're researching? Where does this person fit in the larger context of the story? Who are the people around your subject who might be valuable sources of information? What does your subject do for a living? Is he or she a member of a regulated or licensed profession? Have they had brushes with the law that would have produced court records? What has your subject written that might be publicly available?

Get a file folder with lots of separate pockets, and use them. As you work through the various methods outlined here, you will accumulate records. It's essential that all the paper you gather be kept organized. If you're working on an in-depth project, you'll likely end up with stacks of pages of material whose meaning will become confusing days, weeks, or months later when it comes time to structure and write. Even worse, essential things will get lost. When you come across a valuable website or online article, print it and add it to an electronic folder in your computer. You'll want backups. And keep plenty of written notes on your material to remind you later of what is important. It's also worth creating an electronic spreadsheet file in which you log your phone calls, interviews, documents, and notes. Another useful file is a chronology of your subject's involvement in your story, allowing you to track the evolution of a story over time in a way that your brain simply won't be able to.
Recognize that the more organized and methodical you are in building your file of material, the more useful it will prove to be when you begin to write.

The Internet doesn't make a stupid journalist smart, but it does make a smart journalist smarter. A lot of people just use it as a blind search engine and they don't even use it that well. But if you use it as a finely honed tool, it can be very, very effective.
extracts.notes: Quote from Julian Sher

extracts.extract: General search bars are for amateur researchers. They exist for teens looking for fast access to the latest music video. Not you. Experienced Web searchers need to be more precise, using advanced search options that filter out much of the irrelevant material. All of the major search engines provide instructions for navigating their particular advanced search tools. It's worth learning the skills for several of them. No single search engine covers the whole Internet; most search only a small portion. The more engines you use, the broader your coverage.

The most basic advice I can give for effective Web searching is to be patient. Don't expect a search engine to give you the answers you need in its first few results. While Google's algorithm ranks results by importance, it's quite possible that your dream document doesn't fit into that formula. Go beyond the first few pages of results, and click on anything that looks useful. The answer you need may be buried on the 10th page of results.
extracts.notes: Quote from Astrid Lange, head librarian at the Toronto Star

extracts.extract: Also be aware that search engines like Google and Bing customize your results based on your search history (known as filter bubbles). To avoid this, clear your cookies and cache regularly, or try a search engine like DuckDuckGo which avoids filter bubbles.

The first step is to do some thinking about the question you're asking. Computers are literal. They have no ability to interpret a clumsy array of keywords. It's useful to imagine the ideal document and the words it would contain. Remember that concepts can be expressed in a number of ways; your coming up with an exhaustive list of keywords will help you to unearth documents that would have never turned up in more superficial searches.

It's a way to find someone really specific. If you put orphans, and Africa into a search engine, you'll get everybody. But if you ask for a slide show—who has bothered putting together a slide show and putting it up on a webpage? It's got to be an expert.
extracts.notes: Quote from Julian Sher; refers to filetype: search operator

extracts.extract: The best stories always pull the camera lens back on an issue's big picture. The forest gives the tree context. That's where subject directories come in. These tools give you the kind of fast overview that will give you ideas, resources, and confidence that you're telling a tale that fits within a larger narrative. They're authoritative, often curated, built on a public good instead of a profit model, and are sufficiently broad in scope to serve as a strong starting point for the in-depth research that you won't find by typing a few words into a search engine.

If the site you're looking for has disappeared, it may not be gone forever. The online archiving tool at www.archive.org can help you access websites that have been pulled down. Because it's easy to remove a website from the Internet instantly, it's always a good idea to save a copy of a site onto your hard drive if you know it's going to be important to your research. In some cases, people will pull their sites down once they get a sense that authorities or journalists are looking into their affairs. If you haven't saved a copy, it may be difficult to prove what had been posted previously. The best method here is doing a screen grab of the site by saving an image of what is on screen to your hard drive.

Setting up Internet news alerts is one of the first things Julian Sher does when he begins an investigation. At any given time, he's got at least three or four alerts delivering stories to his email every day. 'If you're working on a long project you want to be able to follow it as it evolves. It gives you leads you wouldn't have thought of because names of people and experts come up.'

There's another kind of expert that journalists often fail to consider. Activists can be incredibly useful sources despite their obvious biases. Using the Internet to get their message out, they provide journalists with gushers of studies, documents, and networks of human sources that could otherwise take weeks to collect. As long as you remain aware of an activist's partiality, the information can still prove useful. Many activists or advocacy groups also collect official records such as government studies and reports, along with newspaper clippings, which come from legitimate and credible sources.

Social media has changed the way we do research because sites like Facebook, Twitter, and LinkedIn allow us to find information about people that we previously could not find. We used to be limited to searching news or legal databases or just the general web to put together a profile on an individual. If a person had not been involved in anything of note, this type of search might not reveal much. But now people post a variety of information on social media allowing us to put together a more robust profile.
extracts.notes: Quote from Astrid Lange

extracts.extract: Privacy settings are often in place to block access to pages of newsmakers, but the search shouldn't stop when a privacy wall appears. Typically, a friends list is accessible, thus providing an instant source list that can be cross-referenced. Clicking on each of those friend pages offers the possibility of some being accessible with comments, shared photos, and insights.

Before you're writing a single word about anyone, you should know everything that has already been written. That means a thorough search of media databases and the Internet. Study previous stories carefully, make notes, and keep the material organized as you work through your story. Look for the unanswered questions in those stories, issues that need to be followed, and names of sources who might be able to help you.

It's also important not to limit yourself too much in terms of the scope of your reading. In the process of producing a documentary and a book on the David Milgaard story in the early 1990s, CBC journalist Cecil Rosner read a year's worth of Saskatoon's The StarPhoenix newspapers prior to the Gail Miller murder. This provided valuable context about the nature of the community, the attitudes to hippies and counter-culture which played a role in police activities, the nature of the police force itself, the attitudes of the police chief, etc. It gave me information I would have never thought to ask about.
extracts.notes: Quote from Cecil Rosner

extracts.extract: Searching media databases can be daunting. Putting a couple of keywords into a database can turn up thousands of hits. So you'll need to develop techniques for limiting such searches to find the major pieces you need to know. Consider limiting the results to stories that are 1000 words or over; this filter will remove small news stories, and focus your search on more in-depth pieces.

Also, consider the less-obvious information published in the media, such as obituaries. Julian Sher recalls the challenge of finding the family of a dead man from Halifax with a common surname. 'We did the standard directory searches in Halifax, then moved outward to Nova Scotia and then the Maritimes. My editor suggested quietly that we do a special search for the obit. Sure enough, in the obit, they mentioned all the next of kin and then we found the mother.'

Be sure to save or copy important stories from media databases, as you will likely need to consult them later. Mark them up. Highlight key facts. Put question marks around issues you need to explore. Circle names of people you'll want to talk to. Spending a few hours—or days—just reading is likely the best way to get your brain thinking critically about how to proceed.

Resumés are the calling cards of the working world. They also document how people choose to represent themselves to potential employers. This is an undervalued resource. It provides a road map of a person's life and career, and is invaluable in any investigation. It is often filed with public applications, and therefore is often publicly available.
extracts.notes: Quote from Cecil Rosner

extracts.extract: In general, it's possible to build a road map of any person's life by thoroughly analyzing public records.
extracts.notes: Quote from Cecil Rosner

extracts.extract: As with other documents, don't assume the information contained in a resumé is true. You should report only information you have verified. Resumés can open up research possibilities, however, and often mention references who can be added to your interview list.

I'm always amazed at how many professions are regulated either by their self-governing bodies or government—investment advisors, funeral directors, architects, dieticians, veterinarians. All accumulate information about members of their profession that you're entitled to see or at least know about. Regulation means the possibility of open and public sanction, and that's gold.
extracts.notes: Quote from Glen McGregor

extracts.extract: After building a database containing every disciplinary decision by the Ontario College of Physicians and Surgeons over a six-year period, the journalists noticed how Bradley's name was among a number of physicians with a troubled medical history in the province. This led them to uncover more details of Bradley's long trail of medical negligence. A remarkable story began to unfold from the records of medical regulatory bodies in three different jurisdictions.
extracts.notes: Toronto Star investigation into Dr. Kenneth Bradley

extracts.extract: Never underestimate the power of a solid paper trail. Even in a story rich with characters and details, documents can play an important role—not just in buttressing your story with key facts (and thus providing some legal protection)—but also by providing some key story-telling nuggets and gems.
extracts.notes: Quote from Julian Sher

extracts.extract: Always try to get hold of the main search police warrant in the case you're investigating. Don't let court officials bully you by insisting you can't have them. Unless they are sealed by court order, you have a right to a copy. Search warrants are a journalist's gold mine. For one thing, they provide a certain legal privilege or immunity because you can quote them to explain what the police are alleging without having to necessarily prove the truthfulness of the allegations. And because police also often meticulously lay out their case, search warrants also become powerful storytelling devices.
extracts.notes: Quote from Julian Sher

extracts.extract: Following the money is always a core mission in the backgrounding of individuals and companies. And that pursuit is often intricately connected to liens and property records. The supplementary information provided by lien records can be as useful as the main details of a person's loan. For instance, you might find the subject's date of birth, address, vehicle make and model, and vehicle identification number, allowing you to do a vehicle record search.

One key trick here was knowing to search, not just for the names of the Ford family members, but for the corporate entities. If we hadn't searched for Deco or Doug Ford Holdings Inc., we never would have found the commercial parcels of land, which are the family's most valuable properties.
extracts.notes: Quote from Greg McArthur, Globe and Mail

extracts.extract: I use land title searches and lien searches all the time, but it's usually for a much simpler purpose—locating people, and their home address, so I can seek them out and interview them. But this research was a great reminder that the documents are so much more important. These sorts of records, and the fine print in those records, often tell a story—and in this case, it was a story that clearly contradicted the narrative being promoted by Toronto's chief magistrate.
extracts.notes: Quote from Greg McArthur

extracts.extract: A name, for example, might prompt a lien search that reveals a home address and business address. A reverse search on the business address might provide the name of a company. The name of the company might prompt a corporate search that turns up other officers and directors who are business partners of the person you're researching. A lien search on the company might provide still more details such as the company's level of debt and list of creditors—more potential sources of information. It might also provide information on property or vehicles that can lead to other material from motor vehicle searches and land-title inquiries.

Whatever your searches turn up, it's important to go back and read documents again, days or weeks after you obtain them. Names, addresses, or company numbers that meant little to you when you first encountered them may suddenly ring bells when you examine them later with the benefit of further research.

The most important thing is to develop a mindset that prompts you to consider all of the potential research possibilities, to find alternative ways of getting information when roadblocks appear, and always to keep digging.

Analysis of 563 Taser firings indicated three-quarters of suspects were unarmed. The data also documented a host of related injuries—including head wounds, burns, and lacerations—linked to a weapon police have touted as less harmful than pepper spray or batons. The figures also revealed a disturbing tendency to painfully jolt suspects after only the most cursory interaction. Far from being a tool to defuse major conflicts that might otherwise have required lethal force, officers repeatedly used the Taser as a compliance tool.
extracts.notes: CP-CBC Taser investigation case study

extracts.extract: It took access-to-information requests and six months of repeated calls to the RCMP to learn that little had been done to verify the electrical output of Tasers. Furthermore, in analyzing the research material the police force used in making decisions, we realized the RCMP had not only relied on some seriously flawed studies, but had accepted information from Taser International at face value.
extracts.notes: CP-CBC Taser investigation case study

extracts.extract: Several experts analyzed the studies and concluded that they were either extremely limited or lacked scientific rigour. We also obtained a test protocol drafted by Taser International, which was valuable in designing our tests. We hired National Technical Systems, an internationally accredited engineering and testing firm, to randomly test more than 40 units of the most popular model. The results were troubling: More than 10 per cent of the guns were either defective or significantly off specifications.
extracts.notes: CP-CBC Taser investigation; commissioned independent lab testing

extracts.extract: The investigative team spent weeks compiling the best picture possible of RCMP Taser use, painstakingly typing numerous incident details from thousands of individual forms into the computer database. This groundbreaking analysis, illustrated with vivid details from telling cases, revealed that RCMP use of the controversial stun guns had spiked to 1000 incidents annually from just 600 in 2005.
extracts.notes: CP-CBC Taser investigation; manual data entry from forms into database

extracts.extract: It allowed us to move beyond the anecdotal evidence to state with a great degree of certainty what was happening. It is a reminder to me that while hunches and isolated reports are valuable, there is no substitute for years' worth of data that paints a reliable picture.
extracts.notes: Quote from Jim Bronskill

extracts.extract: No matter how good you get at digging through documents, trolling databases, and unearthing archival material, a thorough investigation must involve talking to people. You begin dealing with people from the very beginning of a project: experts, tipsters, government bureaucrats, clerks, secretaries, accident witnesses, and so on. In some cases, when not many documents are available, human sources may give you much of your story.
extracts.chapter: Getting People to Talk: The Art of the Interview

extracts.extract: An interview is not much like ordinary conversation at all. An interview is actually a unique verbal form. Mastering the form takes hard work, a dose of psychology, and a willingness to learn from mistakes. An interview is characterized by a delicate balance of power. The interviewer controls the topics; the interviewee controls the information. Conducting an interview effectively is a kind of verbal seduction. A big part of your job is to raise the comfort level of your subject so he or she will want to talk.
extracts.chapter: Getting People to Talk: The Art of the Interview

extracts.extract: A way to get reluctant sources to talk is to be armed with information. The corporate CEO is more likely to speak to you if you already have the guts of the story. It won't be possible for the company to halt your investigation merely by not speaking with you. In fact, you can advance the legitimate argument that it will be in the subject's best interest to speak to you to ensure that the firm's point of view is heard.
extracts.chapter: Getting People to Talk: The Art of the Interview

extracts.extract: Especially when a story contains findings that will cast an organization or person(s) in a poor light, it is important that you make every effort to obtain an interview or statement. If you get nowhere with telephone calls, and emails or direct messages are ignored, you may need to send a letter—best by registered mail because this assures receipt is documented—repeating the request for an interview and outlining the areas you wish to cover in the interview, as well as key allegations or facts to which you are seeking a response.
extracts.chapter: Getting People to Talk: The Art of the Interview

extracts.extract: Some interviews may be only or mainly information (witnesses to a fire or car crash, for example), some might be emotion (a tearful family reunion with a returning soldier or hostage), and others might be accountability (a government minister). The best interviews have all three. And good interviews proceed in that order from information to emotion to accountability. It's pretty hard to start with a beat-up question and then go back and ask for information. The key thing is that you have to determine ahead of time—and prepare your questions accordingly—what mix of information, emotion, and accountability you want.
extracts.chapter: Getting People to Talk: The Art of the Interview
extracts.notes: Quote from Julian Sher

extracts.extract: Sawatsky is one of the few people who has actually studied thousands of interviews to assess what kinds of questions work most of the time, and which ones fail. His basic message is that interviewers should stay in what he calls input mode, asking simple, evocative, open-ended questions that encourage interview subjects to describe and explain, instead of asking aggressive, closed-ended questions that require only that the subject confirm or deny. Sawatsky argues that closed questions, such as 'Were you surprised?' or 'Are you a racist?', while sometimes sounding informed and tough, allow interviewees to answer simply yes or no, then stitch on whatever information they choose.
extracts.chapter: Getting People to Talk: The Art of the Interview

extracts.extract: The most important interviews should be done in person. You can make eye contact and read body language; it is a more human relationship. The best location may be in the person's natural environment, such as an office or home, which may offer evidence of personal or professional interests in the form of mementos, certificates, family pictures, and so on.
extracts.chapter: Getting People to Talk: The Art of the Interview

extracts.extract: A good interview is like a story, with a beginning, a middle, and an end, with high points and other junctures where it is just cruising along. Your strategy probably begins with some sort of icebreaker. Next, you would usually move to factual questions, which will help establish points of agreement with your interviewee. This done, it is time to hike together into more difficult terrain, and tackle contentious questions. It is a lot easier for both parties to do this once they are acquainted. Next, you aim for the peak, presenting your own findings. The key is to get the housekeeping and moderately contentious material out of the way first, in case bringing up an explosive topic prompts your interviewee to suddenly have a pressing appointment.
extracts.chapter: Getting People to Talk: The Art of the Interview

extracts.extract: You can also reduce the chance of prematurely derailing your interview by keeping your questions non-judgmental. Rather than accusing the Wasteglow CEO of illegally burying waste, you should ask incremental, factual questions that get to the point of the illegality without being accusatory. A good beginning might be, 'We have found Document X, which says that Wasteglow buried nuclear waste in the landfill between 2009 and 2014. What do you have to say about this document?' This approach has a greater chance of eliciting an admission than a blunt question.
extracts.chapter: Getting People to Talk: The Art of the Interview

extracts.extract: Silence in an interview is like a vacuum; it's in people's nature to abhor it. The person may fill that awkward silence, and go beyond what they originally intended to say, and even be more honest about it. The pause may draw out the answer your question didn't.
extracts.chapter: Getting People to Talk: The Art of the Interview

extracts.extract: Doing your interviews in the correct sequence is just as important as organizing the flow within each interview. Generally, investigators work from the outside in, advancing through a series of concentric rings until they reach the target of the investigation, at the very centre. You should limit, as much as possible, the chance that word of your activities will reach the centre while you work through the outer rings. The more warning the target has, the easier it is to develop a public-relations strategy to counter your research, or to try to squelch contacts who might speak to you.
extracts.chapter: Getting People to Talk: The Art of the Interview

extracts.extract: In an investigative context, you must record a person's answers in detail. You're not just looking for a few good quotations. You want the context in which the subject says things, the order, the emotional tone of voice, and—often as important as what is said—what is not said. Recording electronically is always the preferred method in any kind of controversial investigation, since it allows you to prove what someone said.
extracts.chapter: Getting People to Talk: The Art of the Interview

extracts.extract: You will want to consider transcribing at least your most important interviews. Transcription alerts you to subtleties that get missed when taking notes. For legally sensitive material—such as when you make allegations about an identified person or corporation—transcription of major interviews is almost mandatory to ensure you have all the points right and are not overlooking something.
extracts.chapter: Getting People to Talk: The Art of the Interview

extracts.extract: Whatever you do in this regard, it is crucial to review each interview after completing it to see how the person's answers have helped supply necessary information and to determine what new questions have been raised.
extracts.chapter: Getting People to Talk: The Art of the Interview

extracts.extract: Avoid using unnamed sources. The public has a right to know who's behind the information and allegations that the media disseminate; this makes sources accountable and lends credibility to what is reported.
extracts.chapter: Getting People to Talk: The Art of the Interview

extracts.extract: If someone absolutely insists on speaking anonymously but you want them on the record, Eric Nalder recommends going back over not-for-attribution portions of an interview, at the time of the interview or later, to see if the subject can be coaxed into letting certain sections be attributed. Once the person has agreed to a few things, you coax more out of them, in a process Nalder calls 'ratcheting.'
extracts.chapter: Getting People to Talk: The Art of the Interview
extracts.notes: Eric Nalder's ratcheting technique

extracts.extract: The first piece of advice would be to avoid email, whenever possible, for substantive interviews. While it may seem convenient, an emailed Q&A is far too open to manipulation, to prepackaged statements, and to media-relations spin. You cannot see or hear the interviewee. Spontaneity is non-existent, and follow-up questions require another email exchange, allowing a chance for the subject to simply end the discussion.
extracts.chapter: Getting People to Talk: The Art of the Interview

extracts.extract: When it comes to the key accountability interviews, you must speak to the people who are truly accountable. Media officers will try to insist on answering your questions themselves. Make no mistake; this is a power struggle. The media person wants to keep the accountable person out of the spotlight. You need the accountable official because readers, viewers, and listeners aren't stupid. They know the difference in heft between the public-relations officer and the head honcho.
extracts.chapter: Getting People to Talk: The Art of the Interview

extracts.extract: Only by developing a good understanding of the numbers can you find the compelling story. Numbers do lie. So it's important to ask questions, and, when possible, do the math yourself.
extracts.chapter: Following the Money: Seeing the Business Angle in Any Story

extracts.extract: Whenever a story includes a question about how a specific policy, law, or government commitment came to be, one of the places we seek the answer is in the listing of meetings with paid influencers.

There's a great Woody Allen quote about 85 per cent of success is just showing up. I kind of think journalism is a bit the same. Most of the good stories come just from bothering to ask the question or, in this case, bothering to see the document.
extracts.notes: Quote from Glen McGregor

extracts.extract: We found one of the many people who had been stunned with a Taser and featured him in our pieces through discovery of his online comments in response to a CBC forum on stun guns.
extracts.notes: CP-CBC Taser investigation; finding affected individuals through online comments

extracts.extract: Many RCMP Taser reports were poorly filled out. In fact, in tracing dates of some serious injuries and deaths reported in the media, we found no official reports of these Taser incidents at all. For several months, submitting the form to headquarters had been optional, meaning gaps in the record.
extracts.notes: CP-CBC Taser investigation; data quality issues in official records

extracts.extract: The most satisfying part of the project was bringing statistical and scientific rigour to a much-debated and highly polarized subject: the RCMP's use of Tasers.
extracts.notes: CP-CBC Taser investigation

extracts.extract: Journalists using a court document must be certain that no court order restricts the right to publish its contents.
extracts.chapter: Publication Bans and Other Restrictions on Court Documents

extracts.extract: Use the author search. Google will separate out documents that list your subject as an author. One of the less used—but highly valuable—searches for your checklist is finding everything the subject of your research has written.
In order to get away from the numbers, you first need to get close to them.
extracts.chapter: Following the money: business angle in investigations
extracts.notes: Paradox attributed to David Stewart-Patterson: must understand numbers to tell human stories

extracts.extract: I never read the press release until I've read the company's financial filings. This prevents me from being diverted by the company's public relations department.
extracts.chapter: Financial filings and corporate disclosure for reporters
extracts.notes: Attributed to Jay Taparia. Read filings before press releases to avoid spin.

extracts.extract: When reviewing key figures in a financial statement, it's important to read the MD&A for reasons explained earlier in this chapter. It's also important to notice the notes that may be attached to the numbers. The notes indicate the section of the report that provides important context. The language in the notes and MD&A tends to be technical and jargon-heavy.
extracts.chapter: Financial filings and corporate disclosure for reporters
extracts.notes: Applied to BlackBerry case study

extracts.extract: Once you have a basic understanding, it's a good idea to run the numbers by an expert who has no vested interest, such as a business professor who studies the company, to help deepen your understanding and perspective.
extracts.chapter: Financial filings and corporate disclosure for reporters

extracts.extract: Financial statements can be seen as tip sheets to be mined for story ideas.
extracts.chapter: Financial filings and corporate disclosure for reporters

extracts.extract: Private businesses are more secretive about their financial performance because, under normal circumstances, they are not obliged to disclose information to the public. The key is finding documents—in addition to insolvency records, the courts, and bankruptcy trustees and receivers—that they don't want you to see.

The Notice of Intention is the beginning of a process that could unfold with the drama that is usually contained in many instances where companies face the prospect of going out of business. Journalists wanting to get the real story should follow the paper trail, which can begin with the OSB, then wind its way to the court file containing more material related to the case, and eventually to interviews with the major players in the drama.
extracts.notes: Office of the Superintendent of Bankruptcy as starting point

extracts.extract: Ninety-nine per cent of business is done behind closed doors. This is your one chance to pull back the veil and see what's going on.
extracts.notes: David Debenham on journalists missing real story by not reading court documents in insolvency cases

extracts.extract: With CanLII, you see the sausage after it has been made. But some of the interesting stuff is the evidence being made in the sausage. The documents on CanLII are basically saying, at the end of the day, this is what the judge decided. Well, that's interesting, but what evidence was he looking at?
extracts.notes: Debenham. Rulings are summaries; the underlying court file has the real story.

extracts.extract: The owner or president of a private company may not want to divulge the degree to which her company is enriching a bank account (legally or illegally), but she may have no choice in a bankruptcy, divorce hearing, or lawsuit. In court, people have to disclose their money.
extracts.notes: Courts as involuntary disclosure mechanism for private companies

extracts.extract: If creditors are constantly owed money, or if payments are continually behind schedule, then that would tell you about the company's ability to make ends meet. And this is why it's helpful to have lists of creditors.

Government departments and agencies that regulate businesses can also be wonderful sources of information. You won't necessarily find out how much money the company is making or losing, but you will discover a thing or two about its products and track record as a corporate citizen, whether it was fined for a particular transgression, or at least inspected.

In addition to determining whether a company is private or public, it's important for you to find out how a particular business is regulated. If a private company makes prescription drugs, for example, then its activities are monitored by Health Canada and the US Food and Drug Administration.
extracts.notes: First step: identify which regulator monitors the company

extracts.extract: When investigating companies that do business in Canada and the United States, it's useful to check with the regulatory authorities and investigative bodies in both countries.
When investigating companies, one of the first questions to ask is whether the workforce, or at least part of it, is unionized.

Some unions use access-to-information and freedom-of-information laws to extract even more data. The union might be concerned about the company's increasing tendency to cut corners and save money by flouting certain safety regulations. To get documented proof of such activities, the union might have pursued inspection reports or correspondence within Transport Canada, the CFIA, or the provincial agriculture ministry.
extracts.notes: Unions as pre-existing FOI requesters who may already hold documents

extracts.extract: A union may not be interested in painting its employer in too positive a light, especially during contract negotiations, so use its information with that in mind.

Keep track of dates when public companies must file their reports with the securities regulators. As soon as the reports are filed, scour them for news, such as lawsuits, lost earnings, or surprising numbers you won't find in news releases. Develop a good rapport with an expert who can help you compare the information contained in the numbers to what the company is saying publicly. The larger the disconnect, the better the story.
extracts.notes: Disconnect between filings and public statements = story

extracts.extract: Get your own information from the regulators, consumers, courts, competitors, and creditors, particularly from people who are angry with the company and therefore won't mind sharing their information, which still needs to be verified.

For the investigative journalist, access requests are a basic research tool. The legislation opens the door for in-depth research into the machinations of government, the reasons why decisions are made, and their impact on the public. If used strategically, information laws can help discover how taxpayer dollars are spent, warn us of public health risks, uncover corruption, and hold elected officials to account.
extracts.chapter: Getting Behind Closed Doors: Using the Information Laws

extracts.extract: Usually, ATI and FOI requests are not useful for reporters working on tight deadlines. It's not generally a tool for breaking news stories. It is a method for pursuing issues that are likely to be as important in a month or two as they are now, stories that go beyond the press release or official comment of the day. And it's a way of pursuing issues that aren't part of the public debate at all, but should be.
extracts.chapter: Getting Behind Closed Doors: Using the Information Laws
extracts.notes: FOI is for slow-burn stories not breaking news

extracts.extract: The first step in obtaining government records is not filing an information request. In fact, filing a formal written request should only happen after exhausting all informal methods of obtaining the records. Simply asking for them is the best initial approach.
extracts.notes: Ask informally first; formal FOI is last resort

extracts.extract: Another important step before filing a formal request is seeing what information has already been released. Completed requests are a matter of public record, and the records themselves are easily accessible, usually through an informal request.
extracts.notes: Check completed FOI requests before filing new ones

extracts.extract: The more informed and detailed your question, the more complete and precise the response will have to be. Filing a well-crafted information request can take a lot of work. You may need details of a government department's record-keeping and managerial processes, or a company's subsidiaries and regulatory background. The more you know about the question, the better your chances of plucking out the most relevant and important records from government files.
extracts.notes: Specificity of request correlates with quality of response

extracts.extract: There are effectively two kinds of ATI requests. Fishing expedition requests are broader in scope and use open-ended language. The surgical approach comes when you know the specific question you want answered, or, even better, the specific documents or records that contain those answers. The more specific the question, the more specific the response must be.
extracts.notes: Two strategies: broad fishing vs. surgical precision

extracts.extract: One of the best ways to do this is to speak to insiders at the particular government department who are willing to help you understand the issues, what records are going to raise alarm bells, and who can give you advice on how to address these. Such people are a great resource for helping to craft a specific request. Their knowledge of a department's paper trail can be immensely valuable.
extracts.notes: Cultivate department insiders to guide FOI wording

extracts.extract: When requesting databases, you should attempt to find out as much as possible about the data, how it's stored, and what fields it contains. From there, you can determine which fields are likely to be protected by legislation and which should be public. That lets you craft a far more specific request, which is likely to succeed. Doing such work up front saves time later.
If the records you are requesting are likely to contain personal information that would be covered by privacy exemptions, then you should specify that you are waiving any demand for that material.
extracts.notes: Proactively waive personal info to speed up processing

extracts.extract: Dividing large requests into several smaller requests helps maximize the bonus search time you receive.
extracts.notes: Each request gets free initial search time; splitting multiplies free hours

extracts.extract: Time and space are two common parameters for limiting requests. If you've asked for 10 years' worth of records, for example, cutting that time frame in half could quickly reduce response time and cost. Likewise, rather than asking for records for the entire province or region, limiting the request to a particular city or rural area should reduce the number of offices involved.

Be careful when you compromise on aspects of the request. If you're looking at how a particular government program has served the public, cutting down a request to six months' worth of records instead of a year's worth might make your resulting analysis vulnerable to criticism for failing to account for special circumstances in that limited period of time.
extracts.notes: Narrowing requests can undermine analytical completeness

extracts.extract: Make sure you understand the limitations you will face when you begin writing. Don't represent your research material as exhaustive when it is not. The records may still form the basis of an important story, but you should acknowledge any shortcomings in your evidence.

Filing for access to information records is often a frustrating waste of time in investigative stories because the requests are seldom completed in time for your deadlines and frequently the material is so heavily redacted as to be useless. Still, it always pays to fire off some requests at the start of your probe.
extracts.notes: Attributed to Julian Sher. File FOI early even if odds are low.

extracts.extract: People—their misdeeds and heroics and struggles—make your story. But documents and the facts they contain can provide much of the scaffolding to build that tale.
extracts.notes: Julian Sher. Documents = scaffolding; people = story.

extracts.extract: In baseball terms, if you get a hit in one out of every three at-bats, you're doing well. The same goes for access. One request might yield a story, another some interesting background material and the third could be a strikeout. I think the best requests flow from intuition. They could come from reading a news item, noting something said by a cabinet minister or following up on a hunch.
extracts.notes: Jim Bronskill. ~33% success rate for FOI requests is normal.

extracts.extract: The media generally make a one-day wonder out of auditor's reports, but they often trigger a flurry of ongoing work behind government scenes. I submitted various requests to a number of federal departments.
extracts.chapter: Memos, briefing notes, reports, and studies
extracts.notes: Bronskill. Auditor reports seed FOI requests for government follow-up records.

extracts.extract: It's important for journalists to see the documents in their entirety and not rely on a selective interpretation. The research material that politicians rely on to make their case can be more textured or even contradictory than they might portray in front of the media.
extracts.chapter: Memos, briefing notes, reports, and studies

extracts.extract: Rather than filing one request for all of the emails, Press says he broke them into five-day time frames for the emails beginning in the days just after the information breach until the official announcement came. This helped cut down the response time and eliminated many of the fees he was originally asked to pay.
extracts.notes: Jordan Press technique for data breach emails at HRSDC

extracts.extract: Press then took a further step with a request for records triggered by his own previous requests for insights into how officials were reacting to the published revelations behind closed government doors. This is a valuable trick often used by seasoned information requesters to track fallout from published stories based on the original records and the political messaging that follows.
extracts.notes: File FOI for government reaction to your own earlier FOI stories

extracts.extract: The Canadian access-to-information request would likely have been useless in this case. There is a two-year delay at the Canadian Foreign Affairs ATI branch, and exemptions are usually far more heavily applied in Canada to withhold records.
extracts.notes: Stanley Tromp. When Canadian ATI is slow, try US FOIA for same bilateral records.

extracts.extract: Usually, an impartial analysis of the findings will be far more textured than typically reassuring government messaging. Testing and data are clinical. But they typically point to a strong human story.

Ideas for these kinds of stories are all around us. Often, such testing is mentioned in news stories that refer to results contained in government press releases. The opportunity for digging deeper lies in obtaining the primary records and conducting first-hand analysis of what they say, talking to experts, and finding the people who are impacted.
extracts.notes: Go past the press release to the underlying data

extracts.extract: Working with inspection records often requires a three-step process: understand how the inspection system works, request the records, then analyze them.

Perhaps the most basic question to ask any public official responsible for protecting the public interest is simply this: When something goes wrong, where do you write it down? Answers to that question will often provide a starting point that will help you understand where to go next.
extracts.notes: Foundational question for any regulatory beat

extracts.extract: How often do officials conduct their inspections? Are there mandatory time frames? Do they call the company ahead of time or make surprise visits? What exactly do they look for? Where do they record what they see? What are the names of those records? What kind of information is in them? When inspectors get back to the office after one of these inspections, what do they do with their findings? Does the information go into a database? If so, what's the database called? Is it public?
extracts.notes: Full checklist of questions to understand an inspection regime

extracts.extract: Because they might contain private information, the idea is to ask for a blank copy of the inspection form or the fields contained in any resulting database. Blank forms will let you see how the department or ministry catalogues its inspection information.
extracts.notes: Request blank forms as a non-sensitive starting point

extracts.extract: Cumulative data might prompt further questions. If you access data that shows problems in several water-testing plants, it would be in the public interest to identify which ones in particular. For this level of detail, where you'd likely be seeking information about private companies or individuals, you'll most certainly have to file formal information requests.
extracts.notes: Aggregate data -> anomalies -> targeted FOI for specifics

extracts.extract: Inspection records, such as those involving unlicensed home daycares, offer a rare glimpse into the appalling conditions some children endure. Publicizing these reports helps to show why change is needed. And public support for change is often the first step in changing government policy.
extracts.notes: Laurie Monsebraaten on the policy impact of publishing inspection records

extracts.extract: While doing preliminary research before an FOI request is always worthwhile, it's essential for FOI requests for data. You need to know how the data you're requesting is gathered and stored in order to ask for it effectively.
extracts.notes: Chad Skelton

extracts.extract: Many government departments release records as PDF files that are difficult to analyze or get into spreadsheet formats. To save time and annoyance, you'll want to do your due diligence on the data, how they're structured, the format, and the fields of information inside. It will help you ask for the records in a specific format and pre-emptively avoid fields with clearly sensitive private information that will hold up the process.
extracts.notes: Specify format in request to avoid unusable PDFs

extracts.extract: When government pushback comes, Skelton advises speaking with the data experts inside the ministry or department to try and clear it directly rather than working through a communications mediator. Public relations folks and FOI coordinators are often not technically literate enough to know how data is maintained and how it can be released.
extracts.notes: Chad Skelton. Talk to data people, not comms people.

extracts.extract: When Skelton filed requests for paper records earlier in his career, the success rate was about one story for every 10 requests. With data requests, the success ratio is the reverse: about 90 per cent. That's because when you finally get the data you're asking for—say salary files, crime data, parking ticket data—it's almost always worth at least a story.
extracts.notes: Chad Skelton. Data FOIs have ~90% story yield vs ~10% for paper.

extracts.extract: Data journalism is made up of three disciplines that are distinct yet interdependent. The first is the traditional skills of computer-assisted reporting, which use data analysis and mapping software to identify newsworthy patterns and trends in data sets. The second discipline is data visualization. The third leg of data journalism is custom Web development, using Internet coding skills to create unique interactive news applications.
extracts.notes: Framework: analysis + visualization + interactive development

extracts.extract: Generally speaking, the more that a database records something that directly affects people, the more likely it is to be the basis for a compelling investigation. The closer the data gets to people's lives, the more impact your story is likely to have.
extracts.chapter: What Makes a Good Data Investigation?
